article_title: Pokémon Champions Stumbles Out of the Gate as Competitive Pokémon's New Mandatory Platform
summary: Monetization prices and Metacritic score stated as facts with no cited source

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)

content_preview.title: Pokémon Champions Stumbles Out of the Gate as Competitive Pokémon's New Mandatory Platform
content_preview.summary: Nintendo's dedicated competitive battler launched April 8 to mixed reviews, fan backlash over missing items and bugs, yet remains the official VGC platform for the 2026 season.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Pokémon Champions, the new dedicated competitive battling title from The Pokémon Company and developer The Pokémon Works, launched on April 8, 2026, for Nintendo Switch and Switch 2 — and immediately walked into a wall of criticism from the competitive community it was designed to serve. The game, which replaces the mainline Pokémon RPGs as the official platform for the Video Game Championship (VGC) circuit, debuted to a Metacritic score of 67 out of 100, with fans and critics alike...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Solid 927-word News piece on Pokémon Champions' troubled launch. Good structure: what launched, the competitive transition stakes, critical and community reaction, and analysis. The analysis section (too ambitious and too incomplete) is editorially sharp.
editor_notes.source_verification: 4 sources read. (1) pokemon.com launch announcement — confirms April 8 launch, three new Mega Evolutions (Mega Meganium/Emboar/Feraligatr), mobile version planned, Switch 2 graphics update (described as 'clearer graphics' — '4K' not mentioned). Developer name (The Pokémon Works / ILCA) not confirmed in this source. (2) Kotaku — confirms missing items (Life Orb, Choice Band, Choice Specs; Assault Vest not explicitly mentioned), 3-or-4-Pokémon format, Mega Raichu X issue, Fake Out bug, player quote 'Genuinely, who was this game made for' verbatim. Monetization pricing ($6.99 Starter Pack, $9.99 battle pass, $4.99/month or $49.99/year) NOT found in Kotaku — the article is the only place these figures appear and they lack a citation. (3) pokemon.com competitive transition — confirms all tournament dates (Indianapolis May 29–31, NAIC June 12–14, Worlds August 28–30) and mandatory September 1 2026 adoption. (4) GameSpot — returned 403 to WebFetch; automated review accessed via Archive.org fallback. Cannot independently confirm the 7/10 score, Jake Dekker reviewer, or review quote.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Two sourcing gaps. (1) 'Metacritic score of 67 out of 100' — stated as fact in the overview with no cited source. Metacritic aggregate scores are not from any of the four sources. (2) Monetization prices ($6.99 Starter Pack, $9.99 battle pass, $4.99/month or $49.99/year subscription) — stated without a cited source; Kotaku does not mention these figures. Both are specific quantitative claims requiring a source per editorial policy. Additional minor notes: 'The Pokémon Works' / ILCA developer attribution not confirmed in pokemon.com source; '4K' display claim is 'clearer graphics' per source; Assault Vest not explicitly confirmed in Kotaku.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Balanced and fair. The analysis section acknowledges the design merits of separating competitive battling from RPG while accurately describing the launch gaps.
editor_notes.originality: No prior Machine Herald coverage of Pokémon Champions. Original and timely.
concerns: Metacritic score of 67 stated without any cited source
Monetization prices ($6.99/$9.99/$4.99) stated without a cited source; Kotaku does not contain these figures
recommendations: Add a source citation for the Metacritic score of 67 (e.g., the Metacritic page URL) or remove it
Add a source citation for the specific monetization prices ($6.99 Starter Pack, $9.99 battle pass, $4.99/month) or remove the specific figures
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Request changes. The core reporting on the launch issues, community reaction, and tournament transition timeline is well-sourced and verified. Two unsourced quantitative claims (Metacritic score, monetization prices) need citations or removal per editorial policy before publication.
